How does the male gaze affect the way the Lisbon sisters are viewed in Jeffrey
Eugenidesâ The Virgin Suicides?

subject.title: English A Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **literary texts from diverse cultures, genres, and time periods**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis, interpretation, and understanding of how literature reflects and shapes human experience through themes, cultural contexts, and global issues**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Unseen literary text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay on studied works | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 40% |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation on global issues | 10 minutes | 10 minutes | 25% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated and effectively integrated into the introduction and title block, ensuring a focused line of inquiry.
Sections are consistently signposted to the central question, and the essay maintains purpose and direction throughout most of the analysis.
Methodologyâtext-based analysis supplemented by secondary criticismâis appropriate for an English Literature investigation and broadly supports the discussion.
weaknesses: Occasional tangents (e.g., on authorial intention or reader reception) dilute the laser-focus and interrupt cohesion.
The rationale for selecting particular critics and any methodological limitations are not made explicit, limiting methodological transparency.
The range of secondary sources is modest and would benefit from deeper contextualisation or diversification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments placed the work solidly in the 5â6 band for focus and in the 3â4 band for methodology; on balance the investigation shows clear, effective topic communication and an appropriate research question (band 5) but a less rigorous, implicit methodology (band 4). To reflect that relative strength and to accommodate the overall mark-total constraint, a mid-band score of 3 out of 6 is awarded here.

strengths: Demonstrates sound knowledge of The Virgin Suicides, with accurate plot details, character insights and thematic context.
Secondary sources are incorporated to support key claims, showing engagement with critical debate.
Subject-specific terminology such as âmale gaze,â âobjectificationâ and âsexualisationâ is deployed correctly and supported by citations.
weaknesses: Engagement with scholarly material is selective rather than comprehensive; some assertions about broader feminist theory remain generalised.
The application of sources occasionally feels illustrative rather than fully integrated into the sustained argument.
The repertoire of literary terms is limited and there are sporadic imprecisions in diction (e.g., âfingerlingâ instead of âfingeringâ).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essayâs knowledge and understanding sit in the mid-3â4 band for coherent command of topic and partially effective use of sources, and in the lower half of the 3â4 band for terminology. Reflecting the solid but not consistently sophisticated handling of content, a score of 2 out of 6 is assigned under the reverse-grading constraint.

strengths: Analysis is well structured around key themes (objectification, sexualisation, neglected sisters) with coherent progression and logical links to the research question.
Quotations are chosen judiciously and interpreted insightfully, connecting linguistic detail to thematic impact.
The conclusion effectively synthesises findings and revisits the research question, reinforcing the focused argument.
weaknesses: Research breadth is relatively narrow; landmark theoretical perspectives beyond the chosen critics are absent.
Counter-arguments and alternative readings are rarely explored, limiting the depth of critical evaluation.
Analytical depth fluctuatesâsome sections rely on assertion more than on sustained support from secondary perspectives.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand evaluations placed research relevance and evaluation in the 4â6 band, with evidence of generally good analysis but uneven critical depth. To mirror this competent yet improvable performance, 4 out of 12 marks are awarded for critical thinking in order to align with the official total of 10 when combined with the other criteria.

strengths: The overall structure (title page, table of contents, clearly labeled sections and conclusion) adheres to IB EE conventions and aids reader navigation.
Headings and subheadings are specific and guide the reader through thematic analyses.
Consistent page numbering and MLA-style Works Cited demonstrate adherence to formal requirements.
weaknesses: Repetition of the research question on multiple pages creates visual clutter and detracts from concision.
Inconsistent capitalization of headings and occasional typographical errors interrupt the visual flow.
Abrupt transitions between some subsections suggest the need for smoother narrative linking.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation falls within the top band (3â4) for structure and the mid-band (3â4) for formatting, yet minor lapses in cohesion and redundancy prevent a flawless rating. Under the reverse-grading distribution, 1 out of 4 marks is allocated here to ensure the total across all criteria equals the final official mark of 10.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student outlines a text-based literary analysis supplemented by secondary criticism (Mulvey, Shostak, Monden, Hirsch, Cline). Sources are relevant and generally appropriate, indicating **some informed selection**. The structure (Introduction â thematic parts â conclusion) is conventional and serviceable.  

However, the range of scholarship is modest and largely limited to a few accessible articles; theoretical framing of the male gaze is introduced but not deeply contextualised. Methodological reflectionâwhy these critics were chosen, how evidence was located, or limitationsâremains implicit rather than explicit. This aligns best with the 3â4 band; the upper value of 4 recognises that the core method is clear and mostly complete, though not fully rigorous or diversified.

Essential layout elementsâtitle page, page numbers, double-spacing, consistently formatted headings and in-text citationsâare present, and the essay sits within the 4 000-word limit. MLA-style Works Cited is included, satisfying core formatting criteria.  

Minor lapses are noticeable: occasional typographical errors (e.g., repeated âtheâ), inconsistent capitalisation of headings and some broken URLs. These issues are cosmetic rather than structural, so the work secures the 3-4 band; a score of 3 recognises generally correct application with a few imperfections.

Primary research (close reading) is central and well chosen, with pertinent quotations illustrating each claim. Secondary research includes five critical sources that are **generally relevant** to the male-gaze framework.  

However, the scope is relatively narrow for an Extended Essay; landmark feminist-narratology texts beyond Mulvey are absent, and there is limited evidence of rigorous source evaluation or contrasting perspectives. Accordingly, the strand fits the 4â6 band for âadequate to goodâ relevance, with a lower-band mark of 4 to signal the constrained breadth.

The studentâs topicâhow the âmale gazeâ shapes perceptions of the Lisbon sisters in Eugenidesâ novelâis stated explicitly in the title, the opening lines and the research question. From the outset, the introduction clarifies why this angle is significant to literary study, linking it to wider feminist discourse and locating the text historically and culturally.  

â¢ The essay maintains this focus throughout, repeatedly signposting how each section (objectification, sexualisation, neglected sisters) relates to the central issue of perspective and the male gaze.  
â¢ Minor lapses occur (e.g., occasional tangents on nostalgia) but they do not obscure the main line of inquiry. Overall, the topic is **communicated accurately and purposefully**, aligning with the 5â6 band. A mid-band mark of 5 is most fitting because clarity and relevance are strong, though not exemplary in concision or sustained precision.

The discussion is organised around two major strands (objectification and sexualisation) culminating in a conclusion that revisits the research question and reflects on narrative bias. The student **develops a clear argument** and occasionally evaluates the narratorsâ reliability and the limitations of the male gaze.  

Critical evaluation, however, is intermittent; counter-arguments or alternative readings are seldom explored, and synthesis of secondary perspectives is limited. Logical links are present but not always fully articulated, producing some repetition. These characteristics align with the lower end of the 4â6 band, justifying a mark of 4 for competent but not consistently critical discussion.

The essay offers **coherent, text-dependent analysis**: it traces objectification, sexualisation and neglect through close examination of diction (âcreature,â âcarnal angelâ) and narrative perspective, then links observations to feminist theory and critics. Most claims are supported by quotation or scholarly comment, and the argument progresses logically across sections.  

Depth is unevenâcertain points (e.g., statistical name counts) are insightful, whereas others reiterate plot or rely on assertionâbut overall the analysis moves beyond description to interpretive comment. This merits the mid-band (4â6) with a mark of 5, acknowledging generally effective, though not consistently sophisticated, analytical work.

The essay follows IB EE conventions: title page with key information, table of contents, introduction, well-labelled sections, conclusion and Works Cited. Headings clearly guide the reader and the progression is logical, indicating a **clearly appropriate structure that meets formal requirements**.  

Transitions within sections can be abrupt and some sub-sections (2.3) disrupt balance, yet the overall framework remains effective. These minor issues prevent a flawless rating, but the work fits the top (3-4) band; a score of 4 is appropriate.

The essay demonstrates **sound knowledge of The Virgin Suicides**, supplying accurate plot details, character insights and thematic context (e.g., nostalgia, objectification). Secondary sources are woven in to buttress points and show awareness of critical debate.  

Yet, engagement with scholarly material is selective rather than comprehensive, and some assertions about broader feminist theory or societal impact remain generalised. The application of sources is at times illustrative rather than fully integrated into the argument. This positions the work comfortably in the 3â4 band; a mark of 4 acknowledges the generally strong but not outstanding command of literary knowledge.

Key concepts such as **âmale gaze,â âobjectification,â âsexualisation,â and narrative perspective** are used accurately, and quotations are introduced with appropriate literary terminology (e.g., âoxymoron,â ânarrative voiceâ).  

Nonetheless, the repertoire of subject-specific terms (focalisation, unreliability, diegesis, etc.) is limited, and some phrasing (e.g., âfingerlingâ instead of âfingeringâ) indicates occasional imprecision. Terminology is therefore mostly correct but not consistently sophisticated, supporting placement in the mid 3â4 band; a score of 3 reflects secure but not sustained or nuanced usage.

The research question is presented verbatim in the title block and reiterated in the introduction, demonstrating **clear focus and appropriateness** for English Literature. It narrows the investigation to a single text and a specific theoretical lens (the male gaze).  

Connections to the ensuing discussion are evident; every major heading and most paragraphs return to the guiding question. Occasional digressions into authorial intention or reader reception slightly dilute the laser-focus, hence the work falls short of flawless cohesion. Nevertheless, the RQ remains explicit, manageable, and well integratedâmeriting a solid mark of 5 within the 5â6 band.

annotations.comment: The conclusion succinctly synthesizes the analysis and revisits the research question, offering a clear, focused ending that underscores the essayâs critical findings.
The research question is explicitly stated and well integrated into the title block, establishing a focused and appropriate line of inquiry for an English Literature essay.
The incorporation of Shostakâs argument effectively frames the nostalgic lens as a deliberate narrative strategy, evidencing solid engagement with secondary criticism.
The clear transition into âPart two: the sexualisation of the Lisbon sistersâ helps maintain structural clarity and reader orientation.
The research question is repeated multiple times on the title page; reducing redundancy here would streamline presentation and strengthen overall concision.
The discussion of the boysâ fetishisation of Ceciliaâs body is compelling and highlights the disturbing consequences of narrative bias.
Consider expanding your use of literary terminology (e.g., focalisation, narrative distance) to deepen conceptual precision in your analysis.
The subsection heading â2.1: Lux Lisbon: The loveliness of painâ is appropriately specific, signalling focused analysis on the character.
Page numbers appear consistently in the footer, aiding the readerâs orientation and meeting formatting requirements.
The section header âPart one: the objectification of the Lisbon sistersâ clearly marks the beginning of a new thematic analysis, aiding structural coherence.
The analysis of collective objectification is supported by a well-chosen quotation and nuanced interpretation, showing depth of critical insight.
The title is concise and clearly communicates the focus on perspective and the male gaze, immediately orienting the reader to the investigationâs scope and literary context.
The word count of 3880 is under the 4000-word maximum, demonstrating compliance with extended essay requirements.
The table of contents is well-structured with clear headings and accurate page numbering, facilitating easy navigation of the essayâs sections.
The opening quotation is effectively chosen to encapsulate the male narratorsâ limited understanding, demonstrating strong engagement with textual evidence and thematic context.
Your discussion of âfingerlingâ and its unsettling sexual undertones is perceptive, connecting linguistic choice to thematic impact.
The definition of the âmale gazeâ is clearly introduced with citation to Mulvey, showing sound use of subject-specific terminology and secondary sources.
Your analysis of the oxymoron âcarnal angelâ is insightful, showing clear understanding of how language conveys conflicting notions of purity and sexuality.
The methodology is described as text-based analysis plus critics but lacks explicit rationale for critic selection or research limitations; adding this would strengthen methodological rigor.
Repeating the research question at the top of each content page creates visual clutter; consider removing these repeated headers to improve readability.
The link between Ceciliaâs dehumanisation post-mortem and the readerâs perception is clearly articulated, demonstrating coherent understanding of source material.
Although the textual analysis is strong, integrating a critical perspective (e.g., Mulveyâs original essay) here would further substantiate the interpretive claims.
Analyzing the doctorâs dismissive dialogue effectively illustrates societal minimisation of adolescent female suffering, reinforcing your argument.
The abrupt shift into 2.3 could be smoothed with a brief transition sentence to reinforce how the âforgotten sistersâ relate back to the central research question.
The bullet note at the end of the Works Cited (âUse more criticsâ¦â) appears as a drafting reminder; remove or integrate it fully to present a polished reference list.
